board.title: Operating Systems
board.author: Julius Boateng
board.description: As computers became more powerful, operating systems became more complex. Abstractions like virtualization, time-sharing, and dynamic scheduling help modern computers run smoothly while hiding significant complexity from end users.

clues.question: Single-tasking microcomputer OS; preceded DOS and became the late-1970s industry standard; short for Control Program for Microcomputers
Linux-based mobile OS developed by Google; has the largest installed base of any operating system
Microkernel OS optimized for resource-constrained mobile devices; dominant in the 2000s before iOS and Android
IBM's flagship Unix OS for POWER architecture servers; short for Advanced Interactive eXecutive
Unix distribution that helped popularize TCP/IP networking; short for Berkeley Software Distribution
Developed by Microsoft and IBM as a replacement for DOS; brought protected memory and preemptive multitasking to many PCs
Single-tasking command-line OS that powered 1980s IBM PCs and early Windows releases; short for Disk Operating System
Unix-based OS released in 1992 by Sun Microsystems for enterprise servers; introduced DTrace and ZFS
Pioneered capacitive multi-touch screens; popularized smartphone app distribution through the App Store
Portable operating system developed at Bell Labs; known for modular design and simple tools
Unix-like microkernel OS designed to teach operating systems internals; central to the Tanenbaum–Torvalds kernel debate
OS family released in 1985 that came to define the PC era; preinstalled on most PCs and later drew antitrust scrutiny
Unix-based OS descended from NeXTSTEP, built for Apple computers
Open-source Unix-like kernel packaged in distributions such as Ubuntu and Debian; powers nearly all top supercomputers
